{"body":"> **TL;DR — tech-debt follow-up to PR #219.** Remove the placeholder `(letter, '')` declarations for `s/l/f/n/p/d` from `src/data/words/levels.ts` by re-encoding `kiss/chill/whiff/bunny/...` as digraphs (`ss/ll/ff/nn/pp/dd`). Magic-e `(e, '')` stays. Audit invariant `levels-vs-data-invariant.test.ts` must remain green.\n\n## Background\n\nPR #219's audit invariant revealed that 28 words across L2/L4/L6/L7/L8 in `src/data/words/curriculum/aus/levelN.json` encode doubled consonants as `(letter, letter) + (letter, '')`:\n\n- L2: `kiss = (k,k) + (i,ɪ) + (s,s) + (s,'')`\n- L4: `chess`, `chill`, `quoll`, `whiff`\n- L7: `bunny`, `daddy`, `fluffy`, `happy`, `jelly`, `puppy`, `recess`, `silly`, `sunny`\n\nThe user noted: **\"kiss is not mute\"** — the doubled `s` is not a silent letter, it should be encoded as a single `ss /s/` digraph, not `(s, s) + (s, '')`. Same logic applies to `ll`, `ff`, `nn`, `pp`, `dd`.\n\nPR #219 declared `(letter, '')` in `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` as a tactical workaround so the audit invariant passes today, but the data is still semantically wrong.\n\n## Proposal\n\nRe-encode doubled-consonant words as digraphs:\n\n| Before                                       | After                                  |\n|----------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------|\n| `kiss = (k,k)+(i,ɪ)+(s,s)+(s,'')`            | `kiss = (k,k)+(i,ɪ)+(ss,s)`            |\n| `chill = (ch,tʃ)+(i,ɪ)+(l,l)+(l,'')`         | `chill = (ch,tʃ)+(i,ɪ)+(ll,l)`         |\n| `whiff = (wh,w)+(i,ɪ)+(f,f)+(f,'')`          | `whiff = (wh,w)+(i,ɪ)+(ff,f)`          |\n| `bunny = (b,b)+(u,ʌ)+(n,n)+(n,'')+(y,iː)`    | `bunny = (b,b)+(u,ʌ)+(nn,n)+(y,iː)`    |\n| `happy = (h,h)+(a,æ)+(p,p)+(p,'')+(y,iː)`    | `happy = (h,h)+(a,æ)+(pp,p)+(y,iː)`    |\n| `daddy = (d,d)+(a,æ)+(d,d)+(d,'')+(y,iː)`    | `daddy = (d,d)+(a,æ)+(dd,d)+(y,iː)`    |\n\nAdd the digraph declarations to `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL`:\n\n- L2: `(ss, s)` — first appears in `kiss`\n- L4: `(ll, l)`, `(ff, f)` — chill, whiff\n- L7: `(nn, n)`, `(pp, p)`, `(dd, d)` — bunny, happy, daddy\n\nThen **remove** the silent doubled-letter declarations added in PR #219 (`(s,'')`, `(l,'')`, `(f,'')`, `(n,'')`, `(p,'')`, `(d,'')`) — they were placeholders.\n\nThe magic-e silent `(e, '')` declarations stay (PR #219), since words like `dance`, `nerve`, `double` legitimately have a silent terminal `e`.\n\n## Out of scope\n\n- Magic-e re-encoding (e.g. should `dance` use `(c,s)+(e,'')` or `(ce,s)`?) — separate question.\n- Adding silent first-letter words (know, lamb, etc.) — tracked in #231.\n\n## Acceptance\n\n- [ ] Re-encode 28 doubled-consonant words across `aus/level2.json`, `level4.json`, `level7.json`\n- [ ] Add digraph declarations to `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL`\n- [ ] Remove placeholder `(letter, '')` declarations for doubled consonants from `levels.ts`\n- [ ] `levels-vs-data-invariant.test.ts` stays green\n- [ ] `curriculum-invariant.test.ts` stays green\n- [ ] Audit other regions (uk, us, br) once their JSON ships\n\n## Related\n\n- PR #219 — added the placeholder `(letter, '')` declarations as a tactical workaround\n- Memory `project_graphemes_by_level_invariant.md` — the rule this work cleans up\n","state":"CLOSED","title":"Tech debt: re-encode doubled consonants as digraphs + remove placeholder (letter,'') decls"}

You are a Context Analyzer for the ce-compound workflow. Your job is to analyze the problem context and produce a YAML frontmatter skeleton, category directory path, suggested filename, and track classification. Return TEXT DATA only — do NOT write any files.

## Problem Being Documented

**Issue #232** in the base-skill repo (a React/TypeScript educational phonics game). Words containing doubled consonants (e.g. `kiss`, `fluffy`, `happy`) were encoded as a single consonant plus a silent letter (`{g:"s",p:"s"},{g:"s",p:""}`) instead of a proper digraph (`{g:"ss",p:"s"}`). The corresponding `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` entries used placeholder "silent doubled" units that didn't reflect how these sounds are actually taught.

**What was fixed:**
- 15 words across L2, L4, L6, L7 curriculum JSON files re-encoded from `letter + silent letter` to single digraph entries
- 6 placeholder "silent doubled X" entries in `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` (levels.ts) replaced with proper digraph entries (ss, ff, ll, nn, dd, pp)
- Test regex anchored in WordSpellSimpleConfigForm test to avoid dd/d ambiguity
- PR #245 merged 2026-04-29, closes #232

**Origin:** PR #219's audit invariant had revealed the encoding issue — placeholder `(letter, '')` declarations were added as a tactical workaround. Issue #232 was filed as tech debt to properly re-encode them as digraphs.

**Files changed:**
- `src/data/words/levels.ts` — GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL declarations
- `src/data/words/curriculum/aus/level2.json` — kiss
- `src/data/words/curriculum/aus/level4.json` — chess, chill, quoll, whiff
- `src/data/words/curriculum/aus/level6.json` — annoy
- `src/data/words/curriculum/aus/level7.json` — 9 words (bunny, daddy, fluffy, happy, jelly, puppy, recess, silly, sunny)
- `src/games/word-spell/WordSpellSimpleConfigForm/WordSpellSimpleConfigForm.test.tsx` — regex anchor

## Supplementary notes from auto memory
Treat as additional context, not primary evidence. Conversation history and codebase findings take priority.

- GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL must match curriculum JSON — levels.ts is reconciled against src/data/words/curriculum/&lt;region&gt;/levelN.json
- WordFilter graphemesAllowed/Required uses (g,p) pairs — Always check both g and p; never just grapheme letter

## Schema Reference

Read this schema.yaml content for enum validation and track classification:

```yaml

    problem_types:
      - build_error

      - runtime_error
      - performance_issue
      - database_issue
      - security_issue
      - ui_bug
      - integration_issue
      - logic_error
  knowledge:

    problem_types:
      - best_practice
      - documentation_gap
      - workflow_issue
      - developer_experience
      - architecture_pattern
      - design_pattern

      - convention

  date: { type: string, pattern: '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' }
  problem_type: { type: enum, values: [build_error, test_failure, runtime_error, performance_issue, database_issue, security_issue, ui_bug, integration_issue, logic_error, developer_experience, workflow_issue, best_practice, documentation_gap, architecture_pattern, design_pattern, tooling_decision, convention] }
  component: { type: enum, values: [rails_model, rails_controller, rails_view, service_object, background_job, database, frontend_stimulus, hotwire_turbo, email_processing, brief_system, assistant, authentication, payments, development_workflow, testing_framework, documentation, tooling] }

You are a Solution Extractor for the ce-compound workflow. Your job is to extract the problem description, symptoms, investigation history, solution details, root cause explanation, and prevention strategies. Return TEXT DATA only — do NOT write any files.

## Problem Being Documented

**Issue #232** in the base-skill repo (React/TypeScript educational phonics game app called BaseSkill).

### Background
PR #219's audit invariant (`levels-vs-data-invariant.test.ts`) revealed that words containing doubled consonants were encoded incorrectly in the curriculum JSON files. Words like `kiss`, `fluffy`, `happy` were encoded as a single consonant plus a silent letter:
```json
{ "g": "s", "p": "s" }, { "g": "s", "p": "" }
```
instead of a proper digraph:
```json
{ "g": "ss", "p": "s" }
```

PR #219 added placeholder "silent doubled X" entries to `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` as a tactical workaround so the audit invariant would pass. But the data was semantically wrong — doubled consonants aren't silent letters, they're digraphs.

### What the fix involved

**1. GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL updates (`src/data/words/levels.ts`)**

6 placeholder "silent doubled" entries were replaced with proper digraph entries:

| Level | Removed | Added |
|-------|---------|-------|
| L2 | `{ g: 's', p: '', name: 'silent doubled s' }` | `{ g: 'ss', p: 's' }` |
| L4 | `{ g: 'f', p: '', name: 'silent doubled f' }` | `{ g: 'ff', p: 'f' }` |
| L4 | `{ g: 'l', p: '', name: 'silent doubled l' }` | `{ g: 'll', p: 'l' }` |
| L6 | `{ g: 'n', p: '', name: 'silent doubled n' }` | `{ g: 'nn', p: 'n' }` |
| L7 | `{ g: 'd', p: '', name: 'silent doubled d' }` | `{ g: 'dd', p: 'd' }` |
| L7 | `{ g: 'p', p: '', name: 'silent doubled p' }` | `{ g: 'pp', p: 'p' }` |

**2. Word re-encoding (curriculum JSON files)**

15 words across 4 levels re-encoded from `letter + silent letter` to single digraph:

- L2: `kiss` — `[k, i, s, s̲]` → `[k, i, ss]`
- L4: `chess`, `chill`, `quoll`, `whiff` — each from 2-entry to 1-entry digraph
- L6: `annoy` — `[a, n, n̲, oy]` → `[a, nn, oy]`
- L7: `bunny`, `daddy`, `fluffy`, `happy`, `jelly`, `puppy`, `recess`, `silly`, `sunny`

Example before/after for `kiss`:
```json
// Before (wrong): letter + silent letter
[
  { "g": "k", "p": "k" },
  { "g": "i", "p": "ɪ" },
  { "g": "s", "p": "s" },
  { "g": "s", "p": "" }
]

// After (correct): single digraph entry
[
  { "g": "k", "p": "k" },
  { "g": "i", "p": "ɪ" },
  { "g": "ss", "p": "s" }
]
```

**3. Test regex anchoring**

The `dd` digraph added to L7 produced a second button matching the unanchored `/d \/d\//` regex in `WordSpellSimpleConfigForm.test.tsx`. Fixed by anchoring with `^` and `$`:
```tsx
// Before: matches both "d /d/" and "dd /d/"
screen.getByRole('button', { name: /d \/d\//i })
// After: matches only "d /d/"
screen.getByRole('button', { name: /^d \/d\/$/i })
```

### Validation
- All 444 invariant tests pass (levels-vs-data + curriculum)
- TypeScript typecheck passes
- No remaining `silent doubled` entries in GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL
- No remaining letter+silent-letter patterns in affected JSON files
- Config form tests pass after regex anchoring
- PR #245 merged 2026-04-29

## Track Classification

This is a **bug track** problem (logic_error) — the data was semantically incorrect (encoding doubled consonants as silent letters instead of digraphs).

## Supplementary notes from auto memory
Treat as additional context, not primary evidence.

- GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL must match curriculum JSON — levels.ts is reconciled against curriculum JSON files (auto memory [claude])
- WordFilter graphemesAllowed/Required uses (g,p) pairs — Always check both g and p; never just grapheme letter (auto memory [claude])

## Your Output (Bug Track Sections)

Return all of the following sections:

1. **Problem**: 1-2 sentence description
2. **Symptoms**: Observable symptoms/behavior
3. **What Didn't Work**: Failed approaches and why (PR #219's tactical workaround)
4. **Solution**: The actual fix with before/after code examples
5. **Why This Works**: Root cause explanation and why the solution addresses it
6. **Prevention**: Strategies to avoid recurrence, best practices, test cases

---

## What was tried before

The issue was first detected as a side effect of issue #216 work (reconciling `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` with curriculum JSON). That master session (`fa681a37`, 2026-04-29) produced an invariant test (`levels-vs-data-invariant.test.ts`) and explicitly flagged doubled-consonant re-encoding as a known follow-up item — it was listed as remaining work but deferred, not tackled.

The dedicated issue #232 session (`feat/issue-232`, 2026-04-29T12:06–13:13) started by fetching the issue from GitHub and immediately cross-referencing the curriculum against the reference word list at `docs/superpowers/plans/2026-04-11-phonic-word-library_words-list.md`. The assistant ran grep-based searches across level6–level8 JSON files to surface all `(letter, "")` encoding pairs and found 15 doubled-consonant words.

A Python audit script was then run across all 8 level JSONs to separate:
- 15 genuine encoding errors (doubled consonants encoded as letter + silent letter instead of digraph)
- 13 magic-e entries (`(letter, "")` for `dance`, `nerve`, etc.) confirmed as correct

The session also confirmed the specific case of `fluffy`: it belongs at level 7 because the terminal `y` encodes as `(y, iː)`, which is a level 7 unit — the `ff` digraph itself was already present in the grapheme pool from level 4 via `whiff`. This framing (level assignment driven by the most advanced grapheme in the word) was used to validate placement decisions across the doubled-consonant words.

## What didn't work

The initial scope was described by the assistant as "15 words" and limited to doubled consonants. The user pushed back twice:

1. First push: asked whether magic-e words (`(e, "")` in `dance`, etc.) also needed fixing — the assistant showed these were intentional and correct.
2. Second push: user expanded scope to a full audit covering grapheme errors, missing words, IPA accuracy, and level assignment — not just silent letter pairs. The assistant had presented only the doubled-consonant subset initially.

The word list was also noted as potentially incomplete — the user redirected the assistant to `phonic-word-library_words-list.md` as the canonical reference, suggesting the assistant's initial enumeration was based solely on the JSON files without cross-checking the authoritative word library.

## Key decisions

- **15 words to re-encode** across levels 2–7: doubled consonants (`ss`, `ff`, `ll`, `nn`, `dd`, `pp`) must be encoded as a single digraph grapheme, not as `(letter, "")`.
- **Magic-e `(e, "")` entries are correct and should not be changed** — they represent a different phonics concept (silent trailing e), not a doubled-consonant encoding error.
- **Approach A chosen**: update `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` in `levels.ts` and re-encode the 15 affected words in their respective level JSON files. Two alternative approaches were presented but the user selected A.
- **`fluffy`'s level 7 placement is correct** — driven by `(y, iː)` not by `ff`; this framing establishes that level assignment follows the highest-level grapheme in the word, not the digraph being fixed.
- **Issue #243 created** to track the broader audit findings (missing words, IPA accuracy, proper names) that were out of scope for #232 but surfaced during the same session.

## Related context

The `levels.ts` invariant (`GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` must match the curriculum JSON) was established in the earlier issue #216 work and enforced by `levels-vs-data-invariant.test.ts`. The doubled-consonant fix must pass this invariant — adding a digraph to `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` at the level where it first appears is required, not optional. The `fa681a37` session confirmed that `whiff` already introduced `ff` at level 4, so any level 4+ word using `ff` as a digraph was consistent with the existing pool.

The fix was split across multiple commits per PR (level6.json patched in one commit, the 9-word level7.json batch in another) — consistent with the project's baby-step commit convention, and reflected in the commit messages visible in the current git log (`fix(word-spell): re-encode annoy as digraph nn in level6.json` and `fix(word-spell): re-encode 9 doubled-consonant words as digraphs in level7.json`).

# Session inventory

Agent-facing primitive. Discover session files and emit session metadata as JSONL across Claude Code, Codex, and Cursor.

This skill exists so that agents researching session history do not need to know the layout of session stores on disk or the JSONL shapes of each platform. The scripts under `scripts/` own that knowledge.

## Arguments

Space-separated positional args:

1. `<repo>` — repo folder name (e.g., `my-project`). Used for directory matching in Claude Code and Cursor, and as the CWD filter for Codex sessions.
2. `<days>` — scan window in days (e.g., `7`). Session files older than this are skipped.
3. `<platform>` *(optional)* — one of `claude`, `codex`, `cursor`. Omit to search all three.
4. `--keyword K1[,K2,...]` *(optional)* — filter to sessions whose full file content matches at least one of the comma-separated keywords (case-insensitive substring). Each emitted session line gains `match_count` and `keyword_matches` ({K: N, ...}) fields, and the `_meta` line gains `files_matched`. Use this instead of rolling per-file `grep -l` calls when ranking many sessions by topical relevance.

## Execution

Run the discovery-plus-metadata pipeline from the skill's own `scripts/` directory:

```bash
bash scripts/discover-sessions.sh <repo> <days> [--platform <platform>] \
  | tr '\n' '\0' \
  | xargs -0 python3 scripts/extract-metadata.py --cwd-filter <repo>
```

To filter by keyword, append `--keyword K1[,K2,...]` to the `extract-metadata.py` invocation. Keyword scanning reads the full file (not just the head metadata window), so it costs more than a metadata-only run — use it when you need to rank candidates by topic across many sessions, not as a default.

Return the raw stdout verbatim — one JSON object per session, then a final `_meta` line. Callers parse the JSONL directly, so do not paraphrase, reformat, or summarize.

If discovery finds no files, the pipeline still emits a clean `_meta` line (`files_processed: 0`). Return that as-is.

## Output format

Each session line is a JSON object. Common fields across platforms:

- `platform` — `claude`, `codex`, or `cursor`
- `file` — absolute path to the session JSONL
- `size` — file size in bytes
- `ts` — session start timestamp (ISO 8601)
- `session` — session identifier

Platform-specific fields:

- Claude Code adds `branch` (git branch) and `last_ts` (last message timestamp).
- Codex adds `cwd` (working directory), `source`, `cli_version`, `model`, `last_ts`.
- Cursor has no in-file timestamps or metadata — `ts` is derived from file mtime and `session` from the containing directory name.

The final `_meta` line has `files_processed`, `parse_errors`, and optionally `filtered_by_cwd` (count of Codex sessions dropped by the CWD filter) and `files_matched` (count of sessions retained by the keyword filter, present only when `--keyword` was set).

When `--keyword` is set, each session line additionally carries:

- `match_count` — total occurrences across all keywords
- `keyword_matches` — per-keyword counts, e.g., `{"middleware": 4, "auth": 12}`

Sessions with `match_count: 0` are excluded from output.

## Error handling

If the discovery script errors (e.g., unreadable home directory, permission failure), let the error surface to the caller. Do not substitute git log, file listings, or other sources — this skill's contract is session metadata, nothing else.

If `_meta` reports `parse_errors > 0`, return the JSONL as-is. The caller decides how to handle partial data.

{"body":"> **TL;DR — tech-debt follow-up to PR #219.** Remove the placeholder `(letter, '')` declarations for `s/l/f/n/p/d` from `src/data/words/levels.ts` by re-encoding `kiss/chill/whiff/bunny/...` as digraphs (`ss/ll/ff/nn/pp/dd`). Magic-e `(e, '')` stays. Audit invariant `levels-vs-data-invariant.test.ts` must remain green.\n\n## Background\n\nPR #219's audit invariant revealed that 28 words across L2/L4/L6/L7/L8 in `src/data/words/curriculum/aus/levelN.json` encode doubled consonants as `(letter, letter) + (letter, '')`:\n\n- L2: `kiss = (k,k) + (i,ɪ) + (s,s) + (s,'')`\n- L4: `chess`, `chill`, `quoll`, `whiff`\n- L7: `bunny`, `daddy`, `fluffy`, `happy`, `jelly`, `puppy`, `recess`, `silly`, `sunny`\n\nThe user noted: **\"kiss is not mute\"** — the doubled `s` is not a silent letter, it should be encoded as a single `ss /s/` digraph, not `(s, s) + (s, '')`. Same logic applies to `ll`, `ff`, `nn`, `pp`, `dd`.\n\nPR #219 declared `(letter, '')` in `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` as a tactical workaround so the audit invariant passes today, but the data is still semantically wrong.\n\n## Proposal\n\nRe-encode doubled-consonant words as digraphs:\n\n| Before                                       | After                                  |\n|----------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------|\n| `kiss = (k,k)+(i,ɪ)+(s,s)+(s,'')`            | `kiss = (k,k)+(i,ɪ)+(ss,s)`            |\n| `chill = (ch,tʃ)+(i,ɪ)+(l,l)+(l,'')`         | `chill = (ch,tʃ)+(i,ɪ)+(ll,l)`         |\n| `whiff = (wh,w)+(i,ɪ)+(f,f)+(f,'')`          | `whiff = (wh,w)+(i,ɪ)+(ff,f)`          |\n| `bunny = (b,b)+(u,ʌ)+(n,n)+(n,'')+(y,iː)`    | `bunny = (b,b)+(u,ʌ)+(nn,n)+(y,iː)`    |\n| `happy = (h,h)+(a,æ)+(p,p)+(p,'')+(y,iː)`    | `happy = (h,h)+(a,æ)+(pp,p)+(y,iː)`    |\n| `daddy = (d,d)+(a,æ)+(d,d)+(d,'')+(y,iː)`    | `daddy = (d,d)+(a,æ)+(dd,d)+(y,iː)`    |\n\nAdd the digraph declarations to `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL`:\n\n- L2: `(ss, s)` — first appears in `kiss`\n- L4: `(ll, l)`, `(ff, f)` — chill, whiff\n- L7: `(nn, n)`, `(pp, p)`, `(dd, d)` — bunny, happy, daddy\n\nThen **remove** the silent doubled-letter declarations added in PR #219 (`(s,'')`, `(l,'')`, `(f,'')`, `(n,'')`, `(p,'')`, `(d,'')`) — they were placeholders.\n\nThe magic-e silent `(e, '')` declarations stay (PR #219), since words like `dance`, `nerve`, `double` legitimately have a silent terminal `e`.\n\n## Out of scope\n\n- Magic-e re-encoding (e.g. should `dance` use `(c,s)+(e,'')` or `(ce,s)`?) — separate question.\n- Adding silent first-letter words (know, lamb, etc.) — tracked in #231.\n\n## Acceptance\n\n- [ ] Re-encode 28 doubled-consonant words across `aus/level2.json`, `level4.json`, `level7.json`\n- [ ] Add digraph declarations to `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL`\n- [ ] Remove placeholder `(letter, '')` declarations for doubled consonants from `levels.ts`\n- [ ] `levels-vs-data-invariant.test.ts` stays green\n- [ ] `curriculum-invariant.test.ts` stays green\n- [ ] Audit other regions (uk, us, br) once their JSON ships\n\n## Related\n\n- PR #219 — added the placeholder `(letter, '')` declarations as a tactical workaround\n- Memory `project_graphemes_by_level_invariant.md` — the rule this work cleans ```text
{"body":"## Background\n\nPR #219 reconciled `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` with the curriculum JSON and added a `levels-vs-data-invariant` test that locks the contract. As part of that work, the silent-letter `(g, '')` pair encoding is now first-class: silent units can be declared in `src/data/words/levels.ts` at the level they first appear, and the picker UI hides `p === ''` chips so silent letters aren't user-toggleable but still participate in cumulative pool matching.\n\nThe existing data only uses `(letter, '')` for **doubled consonants** (kiss, chill, whiff, …) and **magic-e silent e** (dance, nerve, double, …). There are no truly silent first-letter graphemes (silent k, silent w, silent b, silent g, silent h) in the curriculum yet.\n\n## Proposal\n\nAdd a Level 9 (or wherever curriculum design places them) bucket of words that introduce these silent-letter patterns:\n\n- silent k → `know`, `knee`, `knight`, `knife`, `knit`, `knot`\n- silent w → `wrap`, `wrist`, `write`, `wrong`, `wrench`\n- silent b → `lamb`, `comb`, `thumb`, `climb`, `crumb`\n- silent gh → `light`, `night`, `sigh`, `high`, `bright`, `right` (covered by L5 `igh`?)\n- silent g → `sign`, `gnat`, `gnaw`, `design`\n- silent h → `honest`, `hour`, `honor`, `ghost` (some are Level 5 sight words)\n\nEach word would encode the silent letter as `(letter, '')` in its `graphemes` array, and `levels.ts` would declare the pair at the introducing level (e.g. `{ g: 'k', p: '', name: 'silent k' }`).\n\n## Acceptance\n\n- [ ] Decide curriculum placement (L9 or sub-divide existing levels)\n- [ ] Add words to `src/data/words/curriculum/aus/levelN.json`\n- [ ] Add `(letter, '')` declarations to `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL`\n- [ ] `levels-vs-data-invariant.test.ts` stays green\n- [ ] Update region-specific JSON if AUS conventions don't apply\n\n## Related\n\n- PR #219 — the audit invariant + picker UI changes that enable this\n- Memory `project_graphemes_by_level_invariant.md` — the rule this work would extend","labels":[{"id":"LA_kwDORxj_pc8AAAACc5OSJg","name":"enhancement","description":"New feature or request","color":"a2eeef"}],"state":"OPEN","title":"Curriculum: add Level 9 silent-letter words (know, knife, lamb, sign, etc.)"}
```
</details></tool-use>

Get details of issue #178 (DP aligner upgrade)

`gh issue view 178 --json title,body,labels,state 2>/dev/null | head -40`

```text
{"body":"## Background\n\nThe word authoring feature ([spec](../blob/master/docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-23-word-authoring-design.md)) introduces a **greedy left-to-right grapheme→phoneme aligner** at `src/data/words/authoring/aligner.ts` for v1. It picks the longest-matching grapheme that has the highest historical g→p frequency for the next phoneme, falls back to single letters, and absorbs trailing letters into the previous grapheme as silent.\n\nThis is intentionally simple. The chip-edit UI in `AuthoringPanel` exists precisely so curators can fix misalignments, and v1 ships a learning loop: every saved draft becomes a new training point for the corpus.\n\n## Why upgrade\n\nGreedy fails on words where a locally cheap match leads to a globally bad alignment. Examples we expect to see:\n\n- Words with unfamiliar digraphs not seen in the seed corpus.\n- Words where multiple split-digraph patterns compete (e.g., `cake` vs words with adjacent vowels).\n- Words where the phoneme sequence is ambiguous about where syllable boundaries land in letter space.\n\nA **dynamic-programming aligner with corpus-learned costs** would explore all valid (letter-span, phoneme) pairings in O(letters × phonemes × max-grapheme-length) time and pick the alignment with the lowest total cost. Cost = `-log(P(grapheme → phoneme))` from the same `gpFreq` table the greedy aligner already builds.\n\n## Proposed approach\n\n1. Keep the existing `gpFreq` initialization (loads from shipped curriculum at module init).\n2. Add `alignDP(word, phonemes): AlignedGrapheme[]` alongside the greedy `align()`.\n3. DP table: `cost[i][j]` = best cost to align `word[0..i)` to `phonemes[0..j)`.\n4. Transitions: at each `(i, j)`, try consuming 1–4 letters mapping to phoneme `j`, plus split-digraph templates if the pattern matches downstream.\n5. Backtrack to recover the alignment.\n6. Add a feature flag (`USE_DP_ALIGNER` env or a build-time constant) so we can A/B test against greedy on the golden fixtures.\n\n## Acceptance criteria\n\n- All existing `aligner.test.ts` golden fixtures still pass.\n- New tricky-word fixtures (collected from curator corrections in v1) pass.\n- DP path is feature-flagged; default flag flip happens after a measurement window.\n- No regression on bundle size: aligner remains pure TS, no new deps.\n\n## When to do this\n\nAfter v1 of the authoring feature has shipped and accumulated enough curator corrections to make a measurement of greedy's miss rate meaningful. Tracked as a follow-up to keep v1 scope tight.","labels":[{"id":"LA_kwDORxj_pc8AAAACc5OSJg","name":"enhancement","description":"New feature or request","color":"a2eeef"}],"state":"OPEN","title":"Upgrade greedy g→p aligner to DP aligner with corpus-learned costs"}
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-opus-4-6 2026-05-05 15:00:20Z)**_



## 1. Problem

Words containing doubled consonants (e.g., `kiss`, `fluffy`, `happy`) were incorrectly encoded in the curriculum JSON files as a single consonant followed by a silent letter, rather than as a proper digraph. This meant the phonics data was semantically wrong -- doubled consonants are digraphs that produce a single sound, not a letter plus a silent placeholder.

## 2. Symptoms

- PR #219's audit invariant (`levels-vs-data-invariant.test.ts`) flagged mismatches between `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` in `levels.ts` and the actual grapheme-phoneme pairs found in the curriculum JSON files.
- Words like `kiss` were stored as four separate entries `[k, i, s, s(silent)]` instead of three entries `[k, i, ss]`, inflating the grapheme count and misrepresenting the phonics structure.
- `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` contained 6 entries with `name: 'silent doubled X'` (for s, f, l, n, d, p) -- a category that does not exist in English phonics. These entries had `p: ''` (empty phoneme), implying the second consonant is silent, which is incorrect.
- The WordSpell game, which renders one button per grapheme-phoneme pair, would have displayed an extra silent-letter button for every doubled-consonant word -- confusing for students learning phonics.

## 3. What Didn't Work

**PR #219's tactical workaround:** Rather than fixing the data encoding, PR #219 added placeholder `{ g: 's', p: '', name: 'silent doubled s' }` entries to `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` so the audit invariant would pass. This failed because:

- It enshrined an incorrect phonics model. Doubled consonants are not silent letters; they are digraphs (two letters representing one sound). Calling them "silent" teaches the wrong concept.
- It increased the surface area of the workaround -- 6 fake grapheme entries across 4 levels, each needing to be maintained and understood by anyone reading the curriculum data.
- It only satisfied the invariant mechanically (the test passed) without fixing the underlying data, meaning the game would still render these words incorrectly with an extra empty-phoneme button.
- It violated the project's own invariant that `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` must accurately reflect what is in the curriculum JSON -- the entries matched, but both sides were wrong.

## 4. Solution

The fix involved three coordinated changes:

**A. Replace placeholder entries in `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` (`src/data/words/levels.ts`)**

Six "silent doubled" entries were replaced with proper digraph entries:

```typescript
// Before (wrong) — Level 2 example
{ g: 's', p: '', name: 'silent doubled s' }

// After (correct)
{ g: 'ss', p: 's' }
```

All six replacements: `ss` (L2), `ff` (L4), `ll` (L4), `nn` (L6), `dd` (L7), `pp` (L7).

**B. Re-encode 15 words across curriculum JSON files**

Each affected word was changed from a two-entry "letter + silent letter" pattern to a single digraph entry. Example for `kiss` in `level2.json`:

```json
// Before (wrong): 4 entries, last consonant treated as silent
[
  { "g": "k", "p": "k" },
  { "g": "i", "p": "\u026a" },
  { "g": "s", "p": "s" },
  { "g": "s", "p": "" }
]

// After (correct): 3 entries, doubled consonant is one digraph
[
  { "g": "k", "p": "k" },
  { "g": "i", "p": "\u026a" },
  { "g": "ss", "p": "s" }
]
```

The same pattern was applied to all 15 words: `kiss` (L2); `chess`, `chill`, `quoll`, `whiff` (L4); `annoy` (L6); `bunny`, `daddy`, `fluffy`, `happy`, `jelly`, `puppy`, `recess`, `silly`, `sunny` (L7).

**C. Anchor test regex in `WordSpellSimpleConfigForm.test.tsx`**

Adding the `dd` digraph to Level 7 caused a test failure because an unanchored regex `/d \/d\//` matched both the existing `d /d/` button and the new `dd /d/` button:

```tsx
// Before (ambiguous): matches both "d /d/" and "dd /d/"
screen.getByRole('button', { name: /d \/d\//i })

// After (precise): matches only "d /d/"
screen.getByRole('button', { name: /^d \/d\/$/i })
```

## 5. Why This Works

**Root cause:** The original data entry process encoded doubled consonants by splitting them into two separate grapheme-phoneme pairs -- the first with the correct phoneme and the second with an empty phoneme (`p: ""`). This treated the second letter as silent, which is phonetically incorrect. In English phonics, doubled consonants like `ss`, `ff`, `ll` are digraphs: two letters that together represent a single phoneme. They should be encoded as one unit with `g` containing both letters.

**Why the fix addresses it:** By collapsing the two-entry pattern into a single digraph entry (`{ "g": "ss", "p": "s" }`), the data now correctly represents the phonics model. The `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` registry matches with real digraph definitions instead of fake "silent" placeholders. The game renders the correct number of buttons per word (one per actual phoneme, not one per letter), and the audit invariant passes because both sides of the contract -- the level definitions and the curriculum JSON -- agree on the correct encoding.

The regex anchoring fix is a secondary but important correction: when new graphemes are added to a level, any test that matches button labels by substring can produce false ambiguity. Anchoring with `^` and `$` ensures each assertion targets exactly one button.

## 6. Prevention

**Data encoding validation:** The existing `levels-vs-data-invariant.test.ts` now acts as a standing guard -- any new word added to curriculum JSON must have all its grapheme-phoneme pairs present in the corresponding level's `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL`. This prevents silent-letter workarounds from being introduced because the invariant requires a matching entry, and reviewers can reject entries with `p: ''` that do not represent genuinely silent letters.

**Phonics-aware code review checklist:** When reviewing curriculum data changes, verify that doubled consonants (ss, ff, ll, nn, dd, pp, tt, bb, gg, rr, zz, mm, cc) are encoded as single digraph entries `{ "g": "XX", "p": "X" }`, never as two separate entries where the second has an empty phoneme.

**Regex anchoring discipline:** All test assertions that match UI element labels by name should use anchored regexes (`/^...$/`) to avoid ambiguous matches when new graphemes or labels are added. The config form test failure demonstrated how an unanchored regex silently becomes ambiguous when the grapheme set grows.

**No "silent doubled" as a phonics category:** Establish as a project convention that the `name` field in grapheme entries should only use recognized phonics terminology. "Silent doubled X" is not a real category and should be flagged in review as a data modeling error.

**Pair-aware filtering:** Per the project's existing convention, any code that filters or matches graphemes must check both the `g` (grapheme) and `p` (phoneme) fields together, never just the letter. This naturally catches incorrect encodings where the phoneme is unexpectedly empty.

```text
1	# Doubled-Consonant Digraph Re-encoding
2	
3	**Issue:** [#232](https://github.com/leocaseiro/base-skill/issues/232)
4	**Follow-up:** [#243](https://github.com/leocaseiro/base-skill/issues/243)
5	**Date:** 2026-04-29
6	
7	## Problem
8	
9	Words containing doubled consonants (e.g. `kiss`, `fluffy`, `happy`) are
10	encoded as a single consonant plus a silent letter (`{g:"s",p:"s"},{g:"s",p:""}`)
11	instead of a proper digraph (`{g:"ss",p:"s"}`). The corresponding
12	`GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` entries use placeholder "silent doubled" units that don't
13	reflect how these sounds are actually taught.
14	
15	## Scope
16	
17	- Re-encode 15 words across L2, L4, L6, L7
18	- Update `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` to replace placeholder silent entries with digraph
19	  entries
20	- Add one genuinely missing word (`chip`) to L6
21	
22	Out of scope (tracked in #243): missing proper names, L3 review words, full IPA
23	audit, level assignment review.
24	
25	## Changes
26	
27	### GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL updates (`src/data/words/levels.ts`)
28	
29	| Level | Remove                                        | Add                   |
30	| ----- | --------------------------------------------- | --------------------- |
```
</details></tool-use>

 `./docs/superpowers/plans/2026-04-29-wordspell-cumulative-y-filter.md`

```text
1	# WordSpell Cumulative Tile Pool + Y Phoneme Filter — Implementation Plan
2	
3	> **For agentic workers:** REQUIRED SUB-SKILL: Use superpowers:subagent-driven-development (recommended) or superpowers:executing-plans to implement this plan task-by-task. Steps use checkbox (`- [ ]`) syntax for tracking.
4	
5	**Spec:** `docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-29-wordspell-cumulative-y-filter-design.md`
6	
7	**Goal:** Replace PR #219's disjoint chip-filter contract with a two-layer model — cumulative `graphemesAllowed` (so kids always have the tiles they need) + `phonemesRequired` Y filter (so easy pure-prior-level words drop out automatically). Add group-click level headers to replace native checkboxes, unify Simple and Advanced renderings, and split `DEFAULT_WORD_SPELL_CONFIG` so recall mode never carries the picture-mode emoji pool.
8	
9	**Architecture:** Selection state stays as `selectedUnits: LevelGraphemeUnit[]` (PR #219 shape, unchanged). Pure helpers in `level-unit-selection.ts` derive header state and unit-level lookups. `resolveSimpleConfig` derives a `WordFilter` with `graphemesAllowed = cumulativeGraphemes(maxLevel).map(g)` and `phonemesRequired = uniquePhonemes(selectedUnits)`. The UI is a single `WordSpellLibrarySource` component used by both Simple form and Advanced modal slot — chips with clickable level headers (no native `<input type="checkbox">`). A new `WordPreviewBar` queries `filterWords` live below the picker. The route handler splits `DEFAULT_WORD_SPELL_CONFIG` into `DEFAULT_RECALL_CONFIG` (library source, no rounds) and `DEFAULT_PICTURE_CONFIG` (emoji rounds, no source) and `resolveWordSpellConfig` enforces the invariant.
10	
11	**Tech Stack:** TypeScript, React 18, Vitest + React Testing Library, RxDB (for the unchanged migration from PR #219 spec), Storybook 8 (for VR baselines).
12	
13	---
14	
15	## File structure
```
</details></tool-use>

# Session inventory

Agent-facing primitive. Discover session files and emit session metadata as JSONL across Claude Code, Codex, and Cursor.

This skill exists so that agents researching session history do not need to know the layout of session stores on disk or the JSONL shapes of each platform. The scripts under `scripts/` own that knowledge.

## Arguments

Space-separated positional args:

1. `<repo>` — repo folder name (e.g., `my-project`). Used for directory matching in Claude Code and Cursor, and as the CWD filter for Codex sessions.
2. `<days>` — scan window in days (e.g., `7`). Session files older than this are skipped.
3. `<platform>` *(optional)* — one of `claude`, `codex`, `cursor`. Omit to search all three.
4. `--keyword K1[,K2,...]` *(optional)* — filter to sessions whose full file content matches at least one of the comma-separated keywords (case-insensitive substring). Each emitted session line gains `match_count` and `keyword_matches` ({K: N, ...}) fields, and the `_meta` line gains `files_matched`. Use this instead of rolling per-file `grep -l` calls when ranking many sessions by topical relevance.

## Execution

Run the discovery-plus-metadata pipeline from the skill's own `scripts/` directory:

```bash
bash scripts/discover-sessions.sh <repo> <days> [--platform <platform>] \
  | tr '\n' '\0' \
  | xargs -0 python3 scripts/extract-metadata.py --cwd-filter <repo>
```

To filter by keyword, append `--keyword K1[,K2,...]` to the `extract-metadata.py` invocation. Keyword scanning reads the full file (not just the head metadata window), so it costs more than a metadata-only run — use it when you need to rank candidates by topic across many sessions, not as a default.

Return the raw stdout verbatim — one JSON object per session, then a final `_meta` line. Callers parse the JSONL directly, so do not paraphrase, reformat, or summarize.

If discovery finds no files, the pipeline still emits a clean `_meta` line (`files_processed: 0`). Return that as-is.

## Output format

Each session line is a JSON object. Common fields across platforms:

- `platform` — `claude`, `codex`, or `cursor`
- `file` — absolute path to the session JSONL
- `size` — file size in bytes
- `ts` — session start timestamp (ISO 8601)
- `session` — session identifier

Platform-specific fields:

- Claude Code adds `branch` (git branch) and `last_ts` (last message timestamp).
- Codex adds `cwd` (working directory), `source`, `cli_version`, `model`, `last_ts`.
- Cursor has no in-file timestamps or metadata — `ts` is derived from file mtime and `session` from the containing directory name.

The final `_meta` line has `files_processed`, `parse_errors`, and optionally `filtered_by_cwd` (count of Codex sessions dropped by the CWD filter) and `files_matched` (count of sessions retained by the keyword filter, present only when `--keyword` was set).

When `--keyword` is set, each session line additionally carries:

- `match_count` — total occurrences across all keywords
- `keyword_matches` — per-keyword counts, e.g., `{"middleware": 4, "auth": 12}`

Sessions with `match_count: 0` are excluded from output.

## Error handling

If the discovery script errors (e.g., unreadable home directory, permission failure), let the error surface to the caller. Do not substitute git log, file listings, or other sources — this skill's contract is session metadata, nothing else.

If `_meta` reports `parse_errors > 0`, return the JSONL as-is. The caller decides how to handle partial data.

Base directory for this skill: /Users/leocaseiro/.claude/plugins/cache/compound-engineering-plugin/compound-engineering/3.3.2/skills/ce-session-extract

# Session extract

Agent-facing primitive. Extract filtered content from a single Claude Code, Codex, or Cursor session file — either a conversation skeleton or error signals.

This skill exists so that agents do not read multi-megabyte session files into context. The scripts under `scripts/` own the JSONL shape knowledge and emit a narrative-readable digest.

## Arguments

Space-separated positional args:

1. `<file>` — absolute path to a session JSONL file (typically a `file` value returned by `ce-session-inventory`).
2. `<mode>` — `skeleton` or `errors`.
3. `<limit>` *(optional)* — `head:N` or `tail:N` to cap output at N lines (e.g., `head:200`). Omit to return full extraction.

## Execution

**Skeleton mode** — narrative of user messages, assistant text, and collapsed tool-call summaries:

```bash
cat <file> | python3 scripts/extract-skeleton.py
```

**Errors mode** — just error signals:

```bash
cat <file> | python3 scripts/extract-errors.py
```

If `<limit>` is `head:N`, pipe through `head -n N`. If `tail:N`, pipe through `tail -n N`. Apply the limit after the Python script, never before — the `_meta` line is emitted last and a head cap may drop it; that is acceptable when the caller asks for a head cap.

Return the raw stdout verbatim. Do not paraphrase, annotate, or synthesize — the caller does synthesis across multiple sessions.

## What each mode returns

### Skeleton

Narrative output with one logical event per block, separated by `---`:

- User messages (text only, no tool results, framework wrapper tags stripped)
- Assistant text (no thinking/reasoning blocks — those are internal or encrypted)
- Tool call summaries; 3+ consecutive same-name calls are collapsed (e.g., `[tools] 5x Read (file1, file2, +3 more) -> all ok`)

Ends with a `_meta` line: `{"_meta": true, "lines": N, "parse_errors": N, "user": N, "assistant": N, "tool": N}`.

### Errors

One line per error, separated by `---`:

- Claude Code: tool results with `is_error: true`
- Codex: `exec_command_end` events with non-zero exit or non-empty stderr
- Cursor: always empty — Cursor agent transcripts do not log tool results

Ends with a `_meta` line: `{"_meta": true, "lines": N, "parse_errors": N, "errors_found": N}`.

## Error handling

If the file cannot be read, let the error surface to the caller. If `_meta` reports `parse_errors > 0`, return the output as-is — partial extraction is still useful and the caller decides whether to widen the search or deep-dive further.

Help turn ideas into fully formed designs and specs through natural collaborative dialogue.

Start by understanding the current project context, then ask questions one at a time to refine the idea. Once you understand what you're building, present the design and get user approval.

Base directory for this skill: /Users/leocaseiro/.claude/plugins/cache/compound-engineering-plugin/compound-engineering/3.3.2/skills/ce-session-extract

# Session extract

Agent-facing primitive. Extract filtered content from a single Claude Code, Codex, or Cursor session file — either a conversation skeleton or error signals.

This skill exists so that agents do not read multi-megabyte session files into context. The scripts under `scripts/` own the JSONL shape knowledge and emit a narrative-readable digest.

## Arguments

Space-separated positional args:

1. `<file>` — absolute path to a session JSONL file (typically a `file` value returned by `ce-session-inventory`).
2. `<mode>` — `skeleton` or `errors`.
3. `<limit>` *(optional)* — `head:N` or `tail:N` to cap output at N lines (e.g., `head:200`). Omit to return full extraction.

## Execution

**Skeleton mode** — narrative of user messages, assistant text, and collapsed tool-call summaries:

```bash
cat <file> | python3 scripts/extract-skeleton.py
```

**Errors mode** — just error signals:

```bash
cat <file> | python3 scripts/extract-errors.py
```

If `<limit>` is `head:N`, pipe through `head -n N`. If `tail:N`, pipe through `tail -n N`. Apply the limit after the Python script, never before — the `_meta` line is emitted last and a head cap may drop it; that is acceptable when the caller asks for a head cap.

Return the raw stdout verbatim. Do not paraphrase, annotate, or synthesize — the caller does synthesis across multiple sessions.

## What each mode returns

### Skeleton

Narrative output with one logical event per block, separated by `---`:

- User messages (text only, no tool results, framework wrapper tags stripped)
- Assistant text (no thinking/reasoning blocks — those are internal or encrypted)
- Tool call summaries; 3+ consecutive same-name calls are collapsed (e.g., `[tools] 5x Read (file1, file2, +3 more) -> all ok`)

Ends with a `_meta` line: `{"_meta": true, "lines": N, "parse_errors": N, "user": N, "assistant": N, "tool": N}`.

### Errors

One line per error, separated by `---`:

- Claude Code: tool results with `is_error: true`
- Codex: `exec_command_end` events with non-zero exit or non-empty stderr
- Cursor: always empty — Cursor agent transcripts do not log tool results

Ends with a `_meta` line: `{"_meta": true, "lines": N, "parse_errors": N, "errors_found": N}`.

## Error handling

If the file cannot be read, let the error surface to the caller. If `_meta` reports `parse_errors > 0`, return the output as-is — partial extraction is still useful and the caller decides whether to widen the search or deep-dive further.

## What was tried before

The issue was first detected as a side effect of issue #216 work (reconciling `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` with curriculum JSON). That master session (`fa681a37`, 2026-04-29) produced an invariant test (`levels-vs-data-invariant.test.ts`) and explicitly flagged doubled-consonant re-encoding as a known follow-up item — it was listed as remaining work but deferred, not tackled.

The dedicated issue #232 session (`feat/issue-232`, 2026-04-29T12:06–13:13) started by fetching the issue from GitHub and immediately cross-referencing the curriculum against the reference word list at `docs/superpowers/plans/2026-04-11-phonic-word-library_words-list.md`. The assistant ran grep-based searches across level6–level8 JSON files to surface all `(letter, "")` encoding pairs and found 15 doubled-consonant words.

A Python audit script was then run across all 8 level JSONs to separate:
- 15 genuine encoding errors (doubled consonants encoded as letter + silent letter instead of digraph)
- 13 magic-e entries (`(letter, "")` for `dance`, `nerve`, etc.) confirmed as correct

The session also confirmed the specific case of `fluffy`: it belongs at level 7 because the terminal `y` encodes as `(y, iː)`, which is a level 7 unit — the `ff` digraph itself was already present in the grapheme pool from level 4 via `whiff`. This framing (level assignment driven by the most advanced grapheme in the word) was used to validate placement decisions across the doubled-consonant words.

## What didn't work

The initial scope was described by the assistant as "15 words" and limited to doubled consonants. The user pushed back twice:

1. First push: asked whether magic-e words (`(e, "")` in `dance`, etc.) also needed fixing — the assistant showed these were intentional and correct.
2. Second push: user expanded scope to a full audit covering grapheme errors, missing words, IPA accuracy, and level assignment — not just silent letter pairs. The assistant had presented only the doubled-consonant subset initially.

The word list was also noted as potentially incomplete — the user redirected the assistant to `phonic-word-library_words-list.md` as the canonical reference, suggesting the assistant's initial enumeration was based solely on the JSON files without cross-checking the authoritative word library.

## Key decisions

- **15 words to re-encode** across levels 2–7: doubled consonants (`ss`, `ff`, `ll`, `nn`, `dd`, `pp`) must be encoded as a single digraph grapheme, not as `(letter, "")`.
- **Magic-e `(e, "")` entries are correct and should not be changed** — they represent a different phonics concept (silent trailing e), not a doubled-consonant encoding error.
- **Approach A chosen**: update `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` in `levels.ts` and re-encode the 15 affected words in their respective level JSON files. Two alternative approaches were presented but the user selected A.
- **`fluffy`'s level 7 placement is correct** — driven by `(y, iː)` not by `ff`; this framing establishes that level assignment follows the highest-level grapheme in the word, not the digraph being fixed.
- **Issue #243 created** to track the broader audit findings (missing words, IPA accuracy, proper names) that were out of scope for #232 but surfaced during the same session.

## Related context

The `levels.ts` invariant (`GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` must match the curriculum JSON) was established in the earlier issue #216 work and enforced by `levels-vs-data-invariant.test.ts`. The doubled-consonant fix must pass this invariant — adding a digraph to `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` at the level where it first appears is required, not optional. The `fa681a37` session confirmed that `whiff` already introduced `ff` at level 4, so any level 4+ word using `ff` as a digraph was consistent with the existing pool.

The fix was split across multiple commits per PR (level6.json patched in one commit, the 9-word level7.json batch in another) — consistent with the project's baby-step commit convention, and reflected in the commit messages visible in the current git log (`fix(word-spell): re-encode annoy as digraph nn in level6.json` and `fix(word-spell): re-encode 9 doubled-consonant words as digraphs in level7.json`).

# Doubled-consonant words encoded as letter + silent letter instead of digraphs

## Problem

Fifteen words containing doubled consonants across four curriculum levels were
incorrectly encoded in the curriculum JSON files as a single consonant followed
by a silent letter, rather than as a proper digraph. This meant the phonics data
was semantically wrong — doubled consonants are digraphs that produce a single
sound, not a letter plus a silent placeholder.

## Symptoms

- PR #219's audit invariant (`levels-vs-data-invariant.test.ts`) flagged
  mismatches between `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` in `levels.ts` and the actual
  grapheme-phoneme pairs in the curriculum JSON.
- Words like `kiss` were stored as four separate entries `[k, i, s, s(silent)]`
  instead of three entries `[k, i, ss]`, inflating the grapheme count and
  misrepresenting the phonics structure.
- `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` contained 6 entries with `name: 'silent doubled X'` (for
  s, f, l, n, d, p) — a category that does not exist in English phonics. These
  entries had `p: ''` (empty phoneme), implying the second consonant is silent.
- The WordSpell game renders one button per grapheme-phoneme pair, so it would
  have displayed an extra silent-letter button for every doubled-consonant word.

## What Didn't Work

**PR #219's tactical workaround:** Rather than fixing the data encoding, PR #219
added placeholder entries like `{ g: 's', p: '', name: 'silent doubled s' }` to
`GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` so the audit invariant would pass. This failed because:

- It enshrined an incorrect phonics model. Doubled consonants are not silent
  letters; they are digraphs (two letters representing one sound).
- It only satisfied the invariant mechanically (the test passed) without fixing
  the underlying data — the game would still render words incorrectly with an
  extra empty-phoneme button.
- It violated the project's own invariant that `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` must
  accurately reflect curriculum content — both sides of the contract were wrong.

(session history) The issue was first detected during issue #216 work
(reconciling `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` with curriculum JSON). That session produced
the invariant test and explicitly flagged doubled-consonant re-encoding as a
known follow-up item, but deferred it.

(session history) The dedicated #232 session started by cross-referencing
curriculum against the reference word list at
`docs/superpowers/plans/2026-04-11-phonic-word-library_words-list.md`. A Python
audit script was run across all 8 level JSONs to separate 15 genuine encoding
errors (doubled consonants) from 13 correct magic-e entries.

## Solution

The fix involved three coordinated changes across PR #245:

### A. Replace placeholder entries in GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL

Six "silent doubled" entries in `src/data/words/levels.ts` were replaced with
proper digraph entries:

\```typescript
// Before (wrong) — Level 2 example
{ g: 's', p: '', name: 'silent doubled s' }

// After (correct)
{ g: 'ss', p: 's' }
\```

All six replacements: `ss` (L2), `ff` (L4), `ll` (L4), `nn` (L6), `dd` (L7),
`pp` (L7).

### B. Re-encode 15 words across curriculum JSON files

Each affected word was changed from a two-entry "letter + silent letter" pattern
to a single digraph entry. Example for `kiss` in `level2.json`:

\```json
// Before (wrong): 4 entries, last consonant treated as silent
[
  { "g": "k", "p": "k" },
  { "g": "i", "p": "ɪ" },
  { "g": "s", "p": "s" },
  { "g": "s", "p": "" }
]

// After (correct): 3 entries, doubled consonant is one digraph
[
  { "g": "k", "p": "k" },
  { "g": "i", "p": "ɪ" },
  { "g": "ss", "p": "s" }
]
\```

The same pattern was applied to all 15 words:

- **L2:** kiss
- **L4:** chess, chill, quoll, whiff
- **L6:** annoy
- **L7:** bunny, daddy, fluffy, happy, jelly, puppy, recess, silly, sunny

### C. Anchor test regex

Adding `dd` to Level 7 caused an unanchored regex `/d \/d\//` to match both the
`d /d/` and `dd /d/` buttons in `WordSpellSimpleConfigForm.test.tsx`:

\```tsx
// Before (ambiguous): matches both "d /d/" and "dd /d/"
screen.getByRole('button', { name: /d \/d\//i })

// After (precise): matches only "d /d/"
screen.getByRole('button', { name: /^d \/d\/$/i })
\```

## Why This Works

The original data entry process encoded doubled consonants by splitting them into
two separate grapheme-phoneme pairs — the first with the correct phoneme, the
second with an empty phoneme (`p: ""`). This treated the second letter as silent,
which is phonetically incorrect. In English phonics, doubled consonants like
`ss`, `ff`, `ll` are digraphs: two letters that together represent a single
phoneme.

By collapsing the two-entry pattern into a single digraph entry
(`{ "g": "ss", "p": "s" }`), the data now correctly represents the phonics
model. The `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` registry matches with real digraph definitions,
the game renders the correct number of buttons per word (one per actual phoneme),
and the audit invariant passes because both sides of the contract agree on the
correct encoding.

(session history) A key decision during investigation: magic-e `(e, '')` entries
in words like `dance`, `nerve`, `turtle` are correct and should not be changed —
they represent a different phonics concept (silent trailing e), not a
doubled-consonant encoding error.

(session history) Level assignment follows the highest-level grapheme in the
word. For example, `fluffy` belongs at Level 7 because `(y, iː)` is a Level 7
unit — the `ff` digraph itself was already available from Level 4. This framing
validated placement decisions across all the re-encoded words.

## Prevention

- **Invariant test guard:** `levels-vs-data-invariant.test.ts` ensures any new
  word's grapheme-phoneme pairs must be declared in `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL`. This
  prevents workarounds — reviewers should reject entries with `p: ''` that do not
  represent genuinely silent letters (auto memory [claude]).
- **Phonics-aware review:** When reviewing curriculum data changes, verify
  doubled consonants (ss, ff, ll, nn, dd, pp, tt, bb, gg, rr, zz, mm, cc) are
  encoded as single digraph entries `{ "g": "XX", "p": "X" }`, never as two
  separate entries where the second has an empty phoneme.
- **Regex anchoring discipline:** Test assertions matching UI element labels by
  name should use anchored regexes (`/^...$/`) to avoid ambiguous matches when
  new graphemes are added.
- **No "silent doubled" as a phonics category:** The `name` field in grapheme
  entries should only use recognized phonics terminology. "Silent doubled X"
  should be flagged in review as a data modeling error.
- **Pair-aware filtering:** Any code that filters or matches graphemes must check
  both `g` (grapheme) and `p` (phoneme) fields together, never just the letter
  (auto memory [claude]).

26	# Doubled-consonant words encoded as letter + silent letter instead of digraphs
27	
28	## Problem
29	
30	Fifteen words containing doubled consonants across four curriculum levels were
31	incorrectly encoded in the curriculum JSON files as a single consonant followed
32	by a silent letter, rather than as a proper digraph. This meant the phonics data
33	was semantically wrong — doubled consonants are digraphs that produce a single
34	sound, not a letter plus a silent placeholder.
35	
36	## Symptoms
37	
38	- PR #219's audit invariant (`levels-vs-data-invariant.test.ts`) flagged
39	  mismatches between `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` in `levels.ts` and the actual
40	  grapheme-phoneme pairs in the curriculum JSON.
41	- Words like `kiss` were stored as four separate entries `[k, i, s, s(silent)]`
42	  instead of three entries `[k, i, ss]`, inflating the grapheme count and
43	  misrepresenting the phonics structure.
44	- `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` contained 6 entries with `name: 'silent doubled X'` (for
45	  s, f, l, n, d, p) — a category that does not exist in English phonics. These
46	  entries had `p: ''` (empty phoneme), implying the second consonant is silent.
47	- The WordSpell game renders one button per grapheme-phoneme pair, so it would
48	  have displayed an extra silent-letter button for every doubled-consonant word.
49	
50	## What Didn't Work
51	
52	**PR #219's tactical workaround:** Rather than fixing the data encoding, PR #219
53	added placeholder entries like `{ g: 's', p: '', name: 'silent doubled s' }` to
54	`GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` so the audit invariant would pass. This failed because:
55	
56	- It enshrined an incorrect phonics model. Doubled consonants are not silent
57	  letters; they are digraphs (two letters representing one sound).
58	- It only satisfied the invariant mechanically (the test passed) without fixing
59	  the underlying data — the game would still render words incorrectly with an
60	  extra empty-phoneme button.
61	- It violated the project's own invariant that `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` must
62	  accurately reflect curriculum content — both sides of the contract were wrong.
63	
64	(session history) The issue was first detected during issue #216 work
65	(reconciling `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` with curriculum JSON). That session produced
66	the invariant test and explicitly flagged doubled-consonant re-encoding as a
67	known follow-up item, but deferred it.
68	
69	(session history) The dedicated #232 session started by cross-referencing
70	curriculum against the reference word list at
71	`docs/superpowers/plans/2026-04-11-phonic-word-library_words-list.md`. A Python
72	audit script was run across all 8 level JSONs to separate 15 genuine encoding
73	errors (doubled consonants) from 13 correct magic-e entries.
74	
75	## Solution
76	
77	The fix involved three coordinated changes across PR #245:
78	
79	### A. Replace placeholder entries in GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL
80	
81	Six "silent doubled" entries in `src/data/words/levels.ts` were replaced with
82	proper digraph entries:
83	
84	```typescript
85	// Before (wrong) — Level 2 example
86	{ g: 's', p: '', name: 'silent doubled s' }
87	
88	// After (correct)
89	{ g: 'ss', p: 's' }
90	```
91	
92	All six replacements: `ss` (L2), `ff` (L4), `ll` (L4), `nn` (L6), `dd` (L7),
93	`pp` (L7).
94	
95	### B. Re-encode 15 words across curriculum JSON files
96	
97	Each affected word was changed from a two-entry "letter + silent letter" pattern
98	to a single digraph entry. Example for `kiss` in `level2.json`:
99	
100	```json
101	// Before (wrong): 4 entries, last consonant treated as silent
102	[
103	  { "g": "k", "p": "k" },
104	  { "g": "i", "p": "ɪ" },
105	  { "g": "s", "p": "s" },
106	  { "g": "s", "p": "" }
107	]
108	
109	// After (correct): 3 entries, doubled consonant is one digraph
110	[
111	  { "g": "k", "p": "k" },
112	  { "g": "i", "p": "ɪ" },
113	  { "g": "ss", "p": "s" }
114	]
115	```
116	
117	The same pattern was applied to all 15 words:
118	
119	- **L2:** kiss
120	- **L4:** chess, chill, quoll, whiff
121	- **L6:** annoy
122	- **L7:** bunny, daddy, fluffy, happy, jelly, puppy, recess, silly, sunny
123	
124	### C. Anchor test regex
125	
126	Adding `dd` to Level 7 caused an unanchored regex `/d \/d\//` to match both the
127	`d /d/` and `dd /d/` buttons in `WordSpellSimpleConfigForm.test.tsx`:
128	
129	```tsx
130	// Before (ambiguous): matches both "d /d/" and "dd /d/"
131	screen.getByRole('button', { name: /d \/d\//i })
132	
133	// After (precise): matches only "d /d/"
134	screen.getByRole('button', { name: /^d \/d\/$/i })
135	```
136	
137	## Why This Works
138	
139	The original data entry process encoded doubled consonants by splitting them into
140	two separate grapheme-phoneme pairs — the first with the correct phoneme, the
141	second with an empty phoneme (`p: ""`). This treated the second letter as silent,
142	which is phonetically incorrect. In English phonics, doubled consonants like
143	`ss`, `ff`, `ll` are digraphs: two letters that together represent a single
144	phoneme.
145	
146	By collapsing the two-entry pattern into a single digraph entry
147	(`{ "g": "ss", "p": "s" }`), the data now correctly represents the phonics
148	model. The `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` registry matches with real digraph definitions,
149	the game renders the correct number of buttons per word (one per actual phoneme),
150	and the audit invariant passes because both sides of the contract agree on the
151	correct encoding.
152	
153	(session history) A key decision during investigation: magic-e `(e, '')` entries
154	in words like `dance`, `nerve`, `turtle` are correct and should not be changed —
155	they represent a different phonics concept (silent trailing e), not a
156	doubled-consonant encoding error.
157	
158	(session history) Level assignment follows the highest-level grapheme in the
159	word. For example, `fluffy` belongs at Level 7 because `(y, iː)` is a Level 7
160	unit — the `ff` digraph itself was already available from Level 4. This framing
161	validated placement decisions across all the re-encoded words.
162

164	
165	- **Invariant test guard:** `levels-vs-data-invariant.test.ts` ensures any new
166	  word's grapheme-phoneme pairs must be declared in `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL`. This
167	  prevents workarounds — reviewers should reject entries with `p: ''` that do not
168	  represent genuinely silent letters (auto memory [claude]).
169	- **Phonics-aware review:** When reviewing curriculum data changes, verify

171	  encoded as single digraph entries `{ "g": "XX", "p": "X" }`, never as two
172	  separate entries where the second has an empty phoneme.
173	- **Regex anchoring discipline:** Test assertions matching UI element labels by

175	  new graphemes are added.
176	- **No "silent doubled" as a phonics category:** The `name` field in grapheme
177	  entries should only use recognized phonics terminology. "Silent doubled X"

179	- **Pair-aware filtering:** Any code that filters or matches graphemes must check

```text
1	---
2	title: "Doubled-consonant words encoded as letter + silent letter instead of digraphs"
3	date: "2026-04-29"
4	category: logic-errors
5	module: word-spell
6	problem_type: logic_error

8	severity: medium
9	symptoms:
10	  - "Doubled-consonant words (kiss, fluffy, happy) encoded as single consonant + silent letter instead of digraph"
11	  - "GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL contained placeholder 'silent doubled X' entries with empty phonemes"
12	  - "WordSpellSimpleConfigForm test regex matched both 'd' and 'dd' buttons due to unanchored pattern"
13	root_cause: logic_error
14	resolution_type: seed_data_update
15	tags:
16	  - digraph
17	  - grapheme
18	  - phoneme
19	  - curriculum
20	  - doubled-consonant
21	  - data-encoding
22	  - word-spell

24	---
25	
26	# Doubled-consonant words encoded as letter + silent letter instead of digraphs
27	
28	## Problem
29	
30	Fifteen words containing doubled consonants across four curriculum levels were
31	incorrectly encoded in the curriculum JSON files as a single consonant followed
32	by a silent letter, rather than as a proper digraph. This meant the phonics data
33	was semantically wrong — doubled consonants are digraphs that produce a single
34	sound, not a letter plus a silent placeholder.
35	
36	## Symptoms
37	
38	- PR #219's audit invariant (`levels-vs-data-invariant.test.ts`) flagged
39	  mismatches between `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` in `levels.ts` and the actual
40	  grapheme-phoneme pairs in the curriculum JSON.
41	- Words like `kiss` were stored as four separate entries `[k, i, s, s(silent)]`
42	  instead of three entries `[k, i, ss]`, inflating the grapheme count and
43	  misrepresenting the phonics structure.
44	- `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` contained 6 entries with `name: 'silent doubled X'` (for
45	  s, f, l, n, d, p) — a category that does not exist in English phonics. These
46	  entries had `p: ''` (empty phoneme), implying the second consonant is silent.
47	- The WordSpell game renders one button per grapheme-phoneme pair, so it would
48	  have displayed an extra silent-letter button for every doubled-consonant word.
49	
50	## What Didn't Work
51	
52	**PR #219's tactical workaround:** Rather than fixing the data encoding, PR #219
53	added placeholder entries like `{ g: 's', p: '', name: 'silent doubled s' }` to
54	`GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` so the audit invariant would pass. This failed because:
55	
56	- It enshrined an incorrect phonics model. Doubled consonants are not silent
57	  letters; they are digraphs (two letters representing one sound).
58	- It only satisfied the invariant mechanically (the test passed) without fixing
59	  the underlying data — the game would still render words incorrectly with an
60	  extra empty-phoneme button.
61	- It violated the project's own invariant that `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` must
62	  accurately reflect curriculum content — both sides of the contract were wrong.
63	
64	(session history) The issue was first detected during issue #216 work
65	(reconciling `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` with curriculum JSON). That session produced
66	the invariant test and explicitly flagged doubled-consonant re-encoding as a
67	known follow-up item, but deferred it.
68	
69	(session history) The dedicated #232 session started by cross-referencing
70	curriculum against the reference word list at
71	`docs/superpowers/plans/2026-04-11-phonic-word-library_words-list.md`. A Python
72	audit script was run across all 8 level JSONs to separate 15 genuine encoding
73	errors (doubled consonants) from 13 correct magic-e entries.
74	
75	## Solution
76	
77	The fix involved three coordinated changes across PR #245:
78	
79	### A. Replace placeholder entries in GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL
80	
81	Six "silent doubled" entries in `src/data/words/levels.ts` were replaced with
82	proper digraph entries:
83	
84	```typescript
85	// Before (wrong) — Level 2 example
86	{ g: 's', p: '', name: 'silent doubled s' }
87	
88	// After (correct)
89	{ g: 'ss', p: 's' }
90	```
91	
92	All six replacements: `ss` (L2), `ff` (L4), `ll` (L4), `nn` (L6), `dd` (L7),
93	`pp` (L7).
94	
95	### B. Re-encode 15 words across curriculum JSON files
96	
97	Each affected word was changed from a two-entry "letter + silent letter" pattern
98	to a single digraph entry. Example for `kiss` in `level2.json`:
99	
100	```json
101	// Before (wrong): 4 entries, last consonant treated as silent
102	[
103	  { "g": "k", "p": "k" },
104	  { "g": "i", "p": "ɪ" },
105	  { "g": "s", "p": "s" },
106	  { "g": "s", "p": "" }
107	]
108	
109	// After (correct): 3 entries, doubled consonant is one digraph
110	[
111	  { "g": "k", "p": "k" },
112	  { "g": "i", "p": "ɪ" },
113	  { "g": "ss", "p": "s" }
114	]
115	```
116	
117	The same pattern was applied to all 15 words:
118	
119	- **L2:** kiss
120	- **L4:** chess, chill, quoll, whiff
121	- **L6:** annoy
122	- **L7:** bunny, daddy, fluffy, happy, jelly, puppy, recess, silly, sunny
123	
124	### C. Anchor test regex
125	
126	Adding `dd` to Level 7 caused an unanchored regex `/d \/d\//` to match both the
127	`d /d/` and `dd /d/` buttons in `WordSpellSimpleConfigForm.test.tsx`:
128	
129	```tsx
130	// Before (ambiguous): matches both "d /d/" and "dd /d/"
131	screen.getByRole('button', { name: /d \/d\//i })
132	
133	// After (precise): matches only "d /d/"
134	screen.getByRole('button', { name: /^d \/d\/$/i })
135	```
136	
137	## Why This Works
138	
139	The original data entry process encoded doubled consonants by splitting them into
140	two separate grapheme-phoneme pairs — the first with the correct phoneme, the
141	second with an empty phoneme (`p: ""`). This treated the second letter as silent,
142	which is phonetically incorrect. In English phonics, doubled consonants like
143	`ss`, `ff`, `ll` are digraphs: two letters that together represent a single
144	phoneme.
145	
146	By collapsing the two-entry pattern into a single digraph entry
147	(`{ "g": "ss", "p": "s" }`), the data now correctly represents the phonics
148	model. The `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL` registry matches with real digraph definitions,
149	the game renders the correct number of buttons per word (one per actual phoneme),
150	and the audit invariant passes because both sides of the contract agree on the
151	correct encoding.
152	
153	(session history) A key decision during investigation: magic-e `(e, '')` entries
154	in words like `dance`, `nerve`, `turtle` are correct and should not be changed —
155	they represent a different phonics concept (silent trailing e), not a
156	doubled-consonant encoding error.
157	
158	(session history) Level assignment follows the highest-level grapheme in the
159	word. For example, `fluffy` belongs at Level 7 because `(y, iː)` is a Level 7
160	unit — the `ff` digraph itself was already available from Level 4. This framing
161	validated placement decisions across all the re-encoded words.
162	
163	## Prevention
164	
165	- **Invariant test guard:** `levels-vs-data-invariant.test.ts` ensures any new
166	  word's grapheme-phoneme pairs must be declared in `GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL`. This
167	  prevents workarounds — reviewers should reject entries with `p: ''` that do not
168	  represent genuinely silent letters (auto memory [claude]).
169	- **Phonics-aware review:** When reviewing curriculum data changes, verify
170	  doubled consonants (ss, ff, ll, nn, dd, pp, tt, bb, gg, rr, zz, mm, cc) are
171	  encoded as single digraph entries `{ "g": "XX", "p": "X" }`, never as two
172	  separate entries where the second has an empty phoneme.
173	- **Regex anchoring discipline:** Test assertions matching UI element labels by
174	  name should use anchored regexes (`/^...$/`) to avoid ambiguous matches when
175	  new graphemes are added.
176	- **No "silent doubled" as a phonics category:** The `name` field in grapheme
177	  entries should only use recognized phonics terminology. "Silent doubled X"
178	  should be flagged in review as a data modeling error.
179	- **Pair-aware filtering:** Any code that filters or matches graphemes must check
180	  both `g` (grapheme) and `p` (phoneme) fields together, never just the letter
181	  (auto memory [claude]).
182	
183	## Related Issues
184	
185	- [#232](https://github.com/leocaseiro/base-skill/issues/232) — the issue this
186	  fix addresses
187	- [PR #245](https://github.com/leocaseiro/base-skill/pull/245) — the fix PR
188	- [PR #219](https://github.com/leocaseiro/base-skill/pull/219) — origin of the
189	  placeholder workaround
190	- [#243](https://github.com/leocaseiro/base-skill/issues/243) — follow-up audit
191	  (missing words, IPA accuracy, proper names)
192	- [#231](https://github.com/leocaseiro/base-skill/issues/231) — related future
193	  work with genuinely silent letters (know, knife, lamb)
194	- Spec:
195	  `docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-29-doubled-consonant-digraph-reencode-design.md`
196	- Plan:
197	  `docs/superpowers/plans/2026-04-29-doubled-consonant-digraph-reencode.md`
198
```
</details></tool-use>

# Resync

Invoke when you suspect the agent is working against stale state — e.g. it's reading files that look outdated, referencing code that's already been changed upstream, or you want to confirm the current branch is current with `origin/master` before continuing.

## Workflow

Run these steps in order. Be concise — one-line summaries per step.

### 1. Fetch and report branch state

Run in parallel:

```bash
git fetch origin --prune
git rev-parse --abbrev-ref HEAD
git status -sb
git log --oneline HEAD..origin/master
git log --oneline origin/master..HEAD
```

Report in this shape:

- **Branch**: `<current branch>` (or `(HEAD detached at …)`)
- **Behind origin/master by**: N commits
- **Ahead of origin/master by**: N commits
- **Working tree**: clean / M modified, U untracked

If behind > 0 and not on `master`, explicitly ask whether to rebase onto `origin/master`. Do **not** rebase without confirmation. If the working tree is dirty, recommend stashing or committing first.

If on `master`, warn — the project rule is no direct work on `master`; a worktree/branch should be in use.

### 2. List open PRs

```bash
gh pr list --state open --limit 30 --json number,title,headRefName,baseRefName,author,updatedAt,isDraft
```

Summarize in a compact table (number, title, author, branch → base, draft flag, last updated). Keep titles short.

### 3. Flag potentially impacting PRs

Given the conversation's current focus (files being edited, features under discussion), scan the PR list for overlap:

- PRs that touch the same files or directories
- PRs that modify shared infrastructure the current work depends on (reducers, game engine, skin system, config, CI)
- PRs the user has mentioned earlier in the session

For each flagged PR, write one sentence: _"PR #N `<title>` — may impact `<area>` because `<reason>`."_ If nothing obviously overlaps, say so explicitly rather than hedging.

### 4. Confirm before continuing

Ask the user to confirm one of:

- Proceed as-is (current state is fine).
- Rebase onto `origin/master` first.
- Pause this session and review a specific PR first.

Wait for the user's decision. Do not resume prior work until they respond.

## Notes

- Use `gh` for GitHub interactions; don't scrape URLs.
- Don't run `git rebase` or `git pull --rebase` without explicit confirmation — rebases can conflict with uncommitted changes or in-progress worktrees.
- If `gh` is unauthenticated, surface the error and stop at step 2 rather than guessing.
- Keep the whole report under ~25 lines. The user invokes this to re-ground quickly, not to read a novel.

1	// src/data/words/levels.ts
2	import type { Region } from './types';
3	
4	export const ALL_REGIONS: readonly Region[] = ['aus', 'uk', 'us', 'br'];

13	/**
14	 * A single grapheme-phoneme pairing taught at a given level. A grapheme like
15	 * `c` appears twice across the AUS progression — once at level 2 teaching /k/,
16	 * once at level 4 teaching /s/. Modelling each as its own unit keeps both
17	 * variants addressable in the cumulative pool and in filter queries.
18	 */
19	export interface LevelGraphemeUnit {
20	  /** Orthographic form: `c`, `sh`, `igh`, `a_e`. */
21	  g: string;
22	  /** IPA of the sound this unit teaches at this level: `k`, `s`, `ʃ`, `aɪ`. */
23	  p: string;
24	  /** Optional teacher label: `soft c`, `th voiced`. */
25	  name?: string;

131	    { g: 'ou', p: 'ʌ' },
132	  ],
133	};
134	
135	/**
136	 * Returns all LevelGraphemeUnits introduced at or before `level`, preserving
137	 * introduction order and keeping multiple variants of the same orthography
138	 * (e.g. both `c=/k/` and `c=/s/` at level 4+).
139	 */
140	export const cumulativeGraphemes = (
141	  level: number,
142	): LevelGraphemeUnit[] => {
143	  const out: LevelGraphemeUnit[] = [];
144	  const seen = new Set<string>();
145	  for (let l = 1; l <= level; l++) {
146	    for (const unit of GRAPHEMES_BY_LEVEL[l] ?? []) {
147	      const key = `${unit.g}|${unit.p}`;
148	      if (seen.has(key)) continue;
149	      seen.add(key);
150	      out.push(unit);
151	    }
152	  }
153	  return out;
154	};
155	
156	/**
157	 * Deduped orthographic pool for structural word splitting. Used by
158	 * `makeGraphemes` — the splitter only needs to know which grapheme forms
159	 * exist, not which phoneme each one teaches.
160	 */
161	export const graphemePool = (level: number): string[] => {
162	  const seen = new Set<string>();
163	  for (const unit of cumulativeGraphemes(level)) seen.add(unit.g);
164	  return [...seen];
165	};
166
```

python3 -c "
import json
with open('/Users/leocaseiro/Sites/base-skill/src/data/words/curriculum/aus/level2.json') as f:
    data = json.load(f)
# find 'kiss'
for word in data:
    if word.get('word') == 'kiss' or word.get('w') == 'kiss':
        print(json.dumps(word, indent=2))
        break
else:
    # search by looking at graphemes
    for word in data:
        gs = ''.join(pair.get('g','') for pair in word.get('graphemes', word.get('gp', [])))
        if gs == 'kiss':
            print(json.dumps(word, indent=2))
            break
    else:
        # show structure of first item
        print('First item structure:')
        print(json.dumps(data[0], indent=2) if isinstance(data, list) else json.dumps(list(data.items())[:2], indent=2))
"
